context:
This course examines how a variety of cultural traditions propose answers to the question of how to live a meaningful life. It considers the meaning of life, not as a philosophical abstraction, but as a question that individuals grapple with in their daily lives, facing difficult decisions between meeting and defying cultural expectations. The course also provides tools for thinking about moral decisions as social and historical practices, and permits students to compare and contextualize the ways people in different times and places approach fundamental ethical concerns.

Syllabus:
21A.157 The Meaning of Life

Course Meeting Times:
- Lectures: 2 sessions/week; 1.5 hours/session

Prerequisites:
There are no prerequisites for this course.

Course Description:
This course examines how a variety of cultural traditions approach and propose answers to the question of how to live a meaningful life. We consider the meaning of life, not as a philosophical abstraction, but as a matter with which individuals grapple in their daily lives and communities, facing difficult decisions between meeting and defying cultural expectations. The course aims to provide tools for thinking about moral and ethical decisions as social and historical practices, and permits students to compare and contextualize how people in different times and places approach fundamental — though not necessarily universal — questions to do with such topics as family, wealth, self-cultivation, sex, belonging, and powers beyond.

Text:

Required:
Hamdy, Sherine, and Coleman Nye. Lissa: A Story about Medical Promise, Friendship, and Revolution. University of Toronto Press, 2017. ISBN: 9781487593476. [Preview with Google Books]

Suggested:
Kavedžija, Iza, and Harry Walker, eds. Values of Happiness: Toward an Anthropology of Purpose in Life. HAU, 2017. ISBN: 9780986132575.

All other readings can be found in the Readings section.

Grading:

| Activities                            | Percentage |
|---------------------------------------|------------|
| Attendance and participation          | 15%        |
| "Moral Moment" vignette               | 5%         |
| Investigation papers (3 × 20% each)   | 60%        |
| Final paper                           | 20%        |

Course context:
This course examines how a variety of cultural traditions propose answers to the question of how to live a meaningful life. It considers the meaning of life, not as a philosophical abstraction, but as a question that individuals grapple with in their daily lives, facing difficult decisions between meeting and defying cultural expectations. The course also provides tools for thinking about moral decisions as social and historical practices, and permits students to compare and contextualize the ways people in different times and places approach fundamental ethical concerns.

Syllabus:
21A.157 The Meaning of Life

Course Meeting Times:
- Lectures: 2 sessions/week; 1.5 hours/session

Prerequisites:
There are no prerequisites for this course.

Course Description:
This course examines how a variety of cultural traditions approach and propose answers to the question of how to live a meaningful life. We consider the meaning of life, not as a philosophical abstraction, but as a matter with which individuals grapple in their daily lives and communities, facing difficult decisions between meeting and defying cultural expectations. The course aims to provide tools for thinking about moral and ethical decisions as social and historical practices, and permits students to compare and contextualize how people in different times and places approach fundamental — though not necessarily universal — questions to do with such topics as family, wealth, self-cultivation, sex, belonging, and powers beyond.

Text:

Required:
Hamdy, Sherine, and Coleman Nye. Lissa: A Story about Medical Promise, Friendship, and Revolution. University of Toronto Press, 2017. ISBN: 9781487593476. [Preview with Google Books]

Suggested:
Kavedžija, Iza, and Harry Walker, eds. Values of Happiness: Toward an Anthropology of Purpose in Life. HAU, 2017. ISBN: 9780986132575.

All other readings can be found in the Readings section.

Grading:

| Activities                            | Percentage |
|---------------------------------------|------------|
| Attendance and participation          | 15%        |
| "Moral Moment" vignette               | 5%         |
| Investigation papers (3 × 20% each)   | 60%        |
| Final paper                           | 20%        |

Exercise:
TITLE: Final Paper

21A.157 The Meaning of Life
Massachusetts Institute of Technology

Due: Last session
Weight: 20% of the final grade
Length: 1500–1600 words

Assignment:
Go back to the personal moral moment you wrote about in the first weeks of class. Drawing explicitly on (and citing) at least three readings from the semester, and using insights and concepts from the course (e.g., moral trial/laboratory, gender proficiency, achieved/ascribed status, agency (& negative agency), the 'I' and the 'Me', belonging, cultural citizenship, etc.), write an essay analyzing your own story through an anthropological lens.

One possible path is to contextualize the original moment socially, culturally, and/or historically. Another is to establish (by making an argumentative case for) parallels and/or points of contrast with other case studies that we have read. The general idea is to demonstrate what you've learned in the course by giving new meaning to your previous story.

Do not presume that your reader has your previous "moral moment" assignment at hand. Instead, summarize the key players and moments of the story that you're now contextualizing and interpreting.

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: Hi, I’m trying to figure out my final paper for 21A.157. I have my old moral moment, but I’m not sure how to turn it into an anthropological argument with at least three course readings. Can you help me narrow a thesis?
exchanges.tutor: Great, let's work on this together! Before we talk thesis, can you share a quick summary of your moral moment? What happened, who was involved, and what made it feel like a moral decision?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is just starting, so I want to understand what they're working with before guiding them. I should ask them to share their moral moment first, rather than jumping into thesis-building. This follows guided discovery - I need to know the raw material before I can ask productive Socratic questions. Keeping it bite-sized.

exchanges.student: Yeah — mine was about a conflict with my family over my major choice. My parents wanted something more stable/practical, and I wanted something that felt more meaningful to me, so it turned into this choice between belonging and doing what I felt was right for myself. I think it felt moral because it wasn’t just preference — it involved duty, identity, and disappointing people I care about.
exchanges.tutor: That's a rich story with a lot of analytical potential. Looking at the course concepts listed in the assignment — things like the 'I' and the 'Me', achieved vs. ascribed status, belonging, agency — which one do you feel most directly captures what was at stake for you in that moment?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has a rich moral moment with clear themes: duty, identity, belonging, family expectations vs. individual agency. These map well onto course concepts like the 'I' and the 'Me', achieved/ascribed status, belonging, and cultural citizenship. I should use Socratic questioning to help them see these connections themselves rather than pointing them out directly. I'll ask one focused question to get them thinking about which course concepts resonate most with their story.

exchanges.student: I think belonging fits most obviously, and maybe achieved vs. ascribed status too. I’m a little fuzzy on the “I” and the “Me” one though — I kind of remember it being about the self versus society, but I’m not confident. Can you help me sort which of these would actually be strongest?
exchanges.tutor: You're on the right track with the 'I' and the 'Me'. Think about it this way: in your story, was there a tension between what *you* wanted to become and what others *expected* you to be? How does that map onto what you remember about that concept?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to do the analytical work for them - to sort out which concepts are strongest. I should redirect that back to them. But they're also genuinely fuzzy on 'I' and 'Me', which is a legitimate conceptual gap worth addressing through a question rather than explanation. I'll briefly clarify the concept through a question that connects it to their story, which is more useful than a lecture.

exchanges.student: Yeah, I think so. Maybe the “I” is my own impulse or desire, and the “Me” is the version of myself shaped by my family’s expectations? I’m not totally sure if I’m oversimplifying it, but that seems close to what was happening.
exchanges.tutor: That's a reasonable working understanding - good enough to build with. So now you have three potential concepts: belonging, achieved/ascribed status, and the 'I' vs. 'Me'. What do you think the *central tension* of your story is, in one sentence? That tension is usually where a thesis lives.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has made a good intuitive connection. Rather than confirming or correcting in detail, I should affirm the direction and push them to now think about how this connects to their thesis. We've been on concept-clarification for a couple exchanges - time to move toward the argumentative structure. I'll validate briefly and redirect toward thesis-building.

exchanges.student: Maybe the central tension is that choosing a major felt like a personal decision, but it was actually shaped by family expectations and by what kind of person/status I was supposed to become. I don’t know if that’s too descriptive instead of argumentative though.
exchanges.tutor: Good instinct — you're right that it's still descriptive. A thesis makes a *claim* about what that tension reveals or means. So ask yourself: what does analyzing this moment through an anthropological lens show us that a purely personal account wouldn't? What's the bigger insight?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has correctly identified that their statement is more descriptive than argumentative. This is good self-awareness and worth affirming. I should push them to make it argumentative by asking what claim or insight their analysis reveals - what does the anthropological lens *show* that wasn't obvious before? This moves them toward a real thesis without giving it to them.

exchanges.student: I think the bigger insight is that what looked like an individual choice was really a social negotiation about belonging and value. Like, my family wasn’t just giving opinions — they were expressing a cultural idea of what counts as a good life. Is that getting closer to an actual claim?
exchanges.tutor: Yes, that's much stronger — that's a real claim. Now, can you think of a specific reading from the course that supports or illuminates that idea? Which text deals most directly with how cultural definitions of a 'good life' shape individual choices?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has arrived at a genuinely strong thesis seed. This is real progress and worth acknowledging clearly. Now I should push them to sharpen it into an actual thesis sentence and connect it to at least one course reading, moving the conversation toward the paper's structure.

exchanges.student: I feel like *Lissa* could work because it shows people making choices through family, religion, class, and national context, not just individual preference. I’m just not sure if that’s too broad, and I’m blanking a little on what my other two readings should be.
exchanges.tutor: Lissa can absolutely work — the key is using a *specific* moment or character from it, not the whole book. As for other readings, think back: which other texts from the course dealt with family pressure, belonging, or what counts as success or a good life in a cultural context?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has a good instinct about Lissa but is worried it's too broad - worth briefly reassuring that broad texts can be used with specific examples. More importantly, they're blanking on other readings. Rather than suggesting readings for them, I should ask them to think about which themes from their thesis (cultural definitions of good life, belonging, status) connect to other texts they remember from the course. This keeps the discovery process with the student.

exchanges.student: I’m kinda stuck because I don’t remember the full reading list well enough. Could I still build the paper around *Lissa* plus course concepts first, and then figure out the other two readings after?
exchanges.tutor: I'd push back on that — the three readings aren't optional, and building without them risks having to restructure later. What topics do you remember covering in class beyond *Lissa*? Even a theme, a culture, or a case study might jog your memory of a text.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is trying to defer finding the other readings, which is understandable but risks them writing a paper that doesn't meet the minimum requirement of three readings. I should be honest that the three readings are a core requirement and gently push them to engage with their memory or notes now, rather than letting them defer. I can ask a targeted question to help them recall without doing the work for them.

exchanges.student: Okay, fair. I remember we talked about happiness/purpose, family obligations, gender stuff, and belonging/cultural citizenship, but I can’t attach authors to all of that. If I give you the themes I remember, can you help me figure out what kinds of readings would best match my story?
exchanges.tutor: That's really something you need to dig out of your course materials — your syllabus, reading list, or class notes. I'd encourage you to go back to those and look for authors or titles that match the themes you just listed. Once you have a few candidates, bring them here and we can think through how they connect to your argument.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to match readings to their story for them, which crosses into doing their work. I should redirect them to their actual course materials - syllabus, reading list, notes - rather than reasoning from themes. This is a practical boundary: I don't have their reading list, and even if I did, selecting readings for them would undermine the assignment. I should be clear and direct about this.

exchanges.student: Yeah, that makes sense. I’ll check my notes/readings list and come back with a few titles, and then I’d like help deciding which three fit my thesis best.
exchanges.tutor: Good plan. When you come back, try to have a short note on what each text is *about* and why you think it might connect to your thesis. That way we can work through the fit together rather than starting from scratch.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has a good plan and is taking responsibility for the right step. I should affirm this briefly and remind them what to bring back so the next conversation is productive. I should also flag that when they return, I'll help them think through the fit - but won't make the selection for them.
